MINI MINUTES:

Atty. Jeffery G. Nevin of the Nevin Law Firm, Tel. No. 1-510-350-7338 appearing
for the Plaintiff;

RULING - The matter was not argued. The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Plaintiff Anthony Lee's motion to permit late payment of jury fees is granted. Mr. Lee has shown good cause for relief from his failure to pay the jury fees on a timely basis. If Mr. Lee posts the required jury fees by October 17, 2018, Mr. Lee's right to a jury trial is reinstated. Defendants' argument that there are no jury triable issues raised by the parties' pleadings is not amenable to resolution on this motion and must be decided by the trial judge, not a pre-trial judge. "Contract construction does not always present a question of law. Interpretation of a written instrument becomes solely a judicial function only when it is based on the words of the instrument alone, when there is no conflict in the extrinsic evidence, or when a determination was made based on incompetent evidence. But when ... ascertaining the intent of the parties at the time the contract was executed depends on the credibility of extrinsic evidence, that credibility determination and the interpretation of the contract are questions of fact that may properly be resolved by the jury." Coyne v. De Leo (2018) 26 Cal. App. 5th 801, _, quoting City of Hope National Medical Center v. Genentech, Inc. (2008) 43 Cal. 4th 375, 395 (internal citations and quotation marks omitted)). Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR# 8600 =302/hk

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Cross-Complaint Under Anti-Slapp Statute (Code Of Civil Procedure Section 425.16)
Off Calendar Jun-13-2018 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Anthony Lee 415-317-5346 Plaintiff in Pro Per.
No other Appearance.

RULING - The matter is Discussed. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF/CROSS DEFENDANT ANTHONY LEE'S Motion To Strike Cross-Complaint Under Anti-Slapp Statute (Code Of Civil Procedure Section 425.16).

Cross-defendant Anthony Lee's anti-SLAPP motion to strike both causes of action alleged in the cross-complaint filed by Hosie Rice LLP, Spencer Hosie, and Diane Rice is granted as to both causes of action. Mr. Lee satisfied his first prong burden of showing that both causes of action arise from petitioning activity protected by the anti-SLAPP statute. Mr. Lee commenced his action to recover fees on January 3, 2017. Paragraphs 43-44 of the cross-complaint allege that Mr. Lee's e-mail to Hosie Rice on December 29, 2017 and his threat to file a motion for preliminary injunction is the conduct that alleged improperly locked up Hosie Rice's XRP options. Communications during pending litigation implicate petitioning activity protected by the anti-SLAPP statute. (Digerati Holdings LLC v. Young Money Entertainment (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th 873.) Cross-complainants have not satisfied their second prong burden of showing that there is minimal merit to either of their claim. The litigation privilege codified in Civil Code 47 bars liability based on the conduct alleged in paragraphs 43-44 of the cross-complaint. Cross-complainants argue that their claims are also based on communications that Mr. Lee had with Ripple that resulted in the locking the options. This argument fails because the court is obligated to take the cross-complaint "as it is." Cross-complainants cannot "amend" the cross-complaint pleading to defeat this motion. (Premier Medical Management Systems, Inc. v. California Ins. Guarantee Assoc. (2006) 136 Cal.App.4th 464, 476.) "The question is what is pled-not what is proven." (Comstock v. Aber (2012) 212 Cal.App.4th 931, 942.) "Thus, the rule in section 425.16, subdivision (b)(2) that a court must consider affidavits as well as pleadings in the first step of the anti-SLAPP procedure does not provide license to ignore the allegations of a plaintiff's complaint'We therefore conclude that, if the complaint itself alleges acts included within section 425.16, subdivision (e), there is no reason to go beyond the scope of those allegations to determine whether a plaintiff's claims arise from protected conduct." (Bel Air Internet, LLC v. Morales (2018) 20 Cal.App.4th 924, 937.)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Off Calendar Mar-14-2018 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Anthony Lee 415-317-5346 Plaintiff in Pro Per.
Diane Rice, Esq. 415-247-6000 of Hosie Rice LLP for Defendant SPENCER HOSIE, HOSIE RICE LLP, DIANE RICE.

RULING - A R G U E D. Judge Kahn makes a disclosure on the record. There are no objections to the Disclosure. The Court shares the basis for the tentative ruling. Plaintiff argues discrepancy in numbers.(as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentativing ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF ANTHONY LEE's Renewed Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings As To First And Second Causes Of Action.

Plaintiff Anthony Lee's renewed motion for judgment on the pleading as to the first and second causes of action in his second amended complaint is denied as to both causes of action. Upon further review, the court now realizes that the ruling on Mr. Lee's initial motion for judgment on the pleadings erroneously stated that "Mr. Lee correctly calculated the contingency fees owed to him, taking into account the write-offs that defendants' answer alleges should have been taken into account." In making the quoted statement the court only focused on the write-offs alleged in paragraphs 26-29 and 34-36 of the answer to the second amended complaint and failed to consider the write-offs alleged in paragraphs 7, 32 and 39 of that answer. Defendants' re-alleging write-offs of $100,000 or more (separate from the write-offs of $20,184.50 and $12,775) in their amended answer to the second amended complaint does not render the amended answer a sham pleading. In ruling on this motion for judgment on the pleadings, the court is required to accept as true defendants' allegation that they wrote off $100,000 or more and that those write-offs should be taken into account in determining the fees owed to Mr. Lee. Accepting the truth of those allegations requires denying this motion. Moreover, defendants' new factual allegations are sufficient to show that one or more of their affirmative defenses, most notably unclean hands, is a cognizable defense and this is another reason why this motion must be denied.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Notice Of Motion And Plaintiffs Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
Off Calendar Dec-13-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Anthony K. Lee, In Propria Persona (415-317-5346 ) appears as self-represented plaintiff/moving party

No other appearance is made in this matter

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

PLAINTIFF ANTHONY LEE's Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings on the first and second causes of action in the second amended complaint is DENIED without prejudice to refiling another motion for judgment on the pleadings if defendants either do not file a first amended answer by the deadline to do so or the first amended answer fails to sufficiently allege any valid affirmative defense to the first two causes of action. Defendants' request for leave to file a first amended answer providing additional factual allegations to support their affirmative defenses is granted. Defendants must file their first amended answer by December 29, 2017. In their verified answer, defendants admit that they owe Mr. Lee contingency fees for his work in the Backflip and FiTeq cases per their Compensation Agreement, but contend that the calculations in Mr. Lee's second amended complaint do not account for the $20,184.50 and $12,775 write-offs to Backflip and FiTeq. In this motion Mr. Lee correctly calculated the contingency fees owed to him, taking into account the write-offs that defendants' answer alleges should have been taken into account. However, defendants have also pled several affirmative defenses such as unclean hands that may be pertinent to this case. These affirmative defenses are alleged in a conclusory way in keeping with the norm for answers filed in the Bay Area state courts. Usually such conclusory pleading of affirmative defenses is immaterial since the validity of the affirmative defenses rarely affects the outcome of litigation on a pre-trial motion. Because Mr. Lee filed a motion for judgment on the pleadings and established the substantive merit of his first and second causes of action but for the affirmative defenses, the validity of those affirmative defenses is critical to the outcome of this motion. In keeping with the policy of great liberality of permitting amendments to pleadings, especially answers, defendants are given leave, if they can do so in good faith, to allege ultimate facts supporting one or more affirmative defenses that preclude granting the relief sought by Mr. Lee in his first and second causes of action. Written order is signed and filed in open court.
